text: The video shows the trocar configuration setup for the SI robot, specifically arranged for performing a low anterior resection.
The surgical setup includes two 12mm ports on the right side, two 8mm ports on the left side, and a 5mm port positioned between the two 12mm ports on the right side.
The surgeon uses sharp and blunt dissection techniques to separate the small bowel from the retroperitoneum, carefully freeing the bowel from its attachments.
The surgeon transfers the fully mobilized small bowel to the right upper quadrant. Attention is then focused on dissecting the adhesions between the sigmoid colon and the bladder.
The surgeon completes the division of adhesions between the sigmoid colon and the bladder, gaining access into the pelvis at the peritoneal reflection.
The surgeon straightens the sigmoid colon and its mesentery, applying appropriate tension before initiating a medial to lateral dissection.
The surgeon focuses on the mesentery of the sigmoid colon, preparing for further dissection in the pelvic region.
The surgeon initiates a medial to lateral dissection using sharp and blunt techniques within the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, focusing on separating tissue layers in the pelvic region.
The surgeon performs a dissection in the correct plane, which is relatively hemostatic, indicating minimal bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to locate the ureter during the medial to lateral dissection but is unable to visually identify it within the surgical field.
The surgeon focuses on the lateral aspect of the bowel, preparing for further dissection in this area.
The surgeon dissects and removes lateral adhesions from the bowel until reaching the medial dissection plane, ensuring continuity between the two dissection approaches.
The bowel is identified by its characteristic vermiculation, visible as rhythmic contractions along its surface.
The surgeon identifies and protects the ureter, ensuring it is safely positioned away from the surgical field.
The surgeon focuses on the mesentery of the colon, using a vessel sealer to ligate the mesenteric vessels, including the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon dissects the mesentery at a level where there is no visible gross disease, revealing the proximal portion of the bowel, which appears soft.
The surgeon clears the fat around the colon to ensure hemostasis before stapling the proximal portion of the specimen, following the complete dissection of the mesentery up to the colon.
The surgeon focuses on the distal portion of the specimen, preparing for further dissection or manipulation.
The surgeon focuses on the distal portion of the specimen at the rectosigmoid junction, using careful manipulation to identify the rectum.
The surgeon takes the mesentery of the rectum at the predetermined level, ensuring hemostasis is maintained in preparation for subsequent stapling.
The third arm of the surgical apparatus applies cephalad retraction to maintain tension, facilitating adequate dissection and exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to transect the bowel after completing the dissection of the distal specimen, ensuring precise separation of the bowel segment.
The surgeon prepares to use a robotic stapler with a green load, suitable for thicker tissue, to transect the bowel at the distal portion of the specimen.
The surgeon inserts the anvil of an end-to-end anastomosis (EA) stapler through a 12mm port in the right lower quadrant, positioning it within the peritoneal cavity.
An O-silk suture is visibly attached to the plastic head of the anvil, which is positioned within the surgical field.
The surgeon creates a colotomy in the bowel specimen and positions the anvil of the EA stapler past the intended transection site.
The surgeon extends the colotomy in the thickened bowel wall due to disease, allowing the head of the anvil to pass proximally through the incision.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to transect the proximal portion of the specimen, completing the separation of the tissue.
The surgeon extracts the specimen through the right lower quadrant twelve millimeter trocar, removing it from the peritoneal cavity.
A gel port mini is visibly placed at the extraction site in the right lower quadrant, serving as a wound protector.
The surgeon uses a needle driver to pull the suture through the proximal bowel, threading the end of the anvil through the visible staple line.
The surgeon uses a clip-up layer to grasp the end of the anvil and position it appropriately within the surgical field.
The surgeon inserts the EEA stapler into the end of the anvil, which is held in position by the robotic system, completing the anastomosis setup.
The surgeon closes and fires the EEA stapler, completing the anastomosis, and then extracts the stapler from the anus.
The surgeon performs an air leak test by insufflating the bowel, observing no bubbles, indicating a successful seal.
The patient did well post-operatively and was discharged on post-operative day two.
